questions.scenario: A budget alert fires at 85 percent of the monthly forecast for `rg-devpolaris-orders-prod`. What does that alert do by itself?

options.text: It notifies the team so they can investigate; it does not automatically stop resources from running.
It shuts down all resources in the scope when the threshold is crossed.
It proves every resource in the scope is waste.
It restores deleted blobs because budget alerts are recovery tools.

questions.explanation: Budgets are guardrails and notification tools. They are not circuit breakers that automatically stop Azure consumption.

questions.scenario: Azure Advisor recommends downsizing an Azure SQL database because average CPU is low. The database handles a weekly reconciliation job and checkout peaks during campaigns.

options.text: Review peak windows, latency, query pressure, storage growth, and recovery promise before changing the tier.
Apply the recommendation immediately because average CPU is the only safety signal.
Ignore all Advisor recommendations forever because they can never help.
Delete the database backup first so CPU gets lower.

questions.explanation: Advisor can point at likely waste, but a team still needs workload context. Averages can hide peaks, batch jobs, latency needs, and recovery requirements.

questions.scenario: Quiet-hour App Service metrics show CPU below 15 percent for weeks. The service still has traffic spikes during release announcements.

options.text: Right-size only after comparing quiet hours with peaks, latency, scale rules, and rollback evidence.
Scale to the quiet-hour minimum permanently because quiet hours are the whole workload.
Turn off monitoring so low CPU is easier to prove.
Move all cost to Blob Storage because Blob Storage is always cheaper than compute.

questions.explanation: Right-sizing chooses the smallest shape that still protects the service promise. Quiet-hour utilization matters, but peak traffic and recovery behavior matter too.

questions.scenario: Which signals should be in a safe right-sizing review?

options.text: Cost movement by service, resource, tag, and time window.
Traffic, latency, errors, CPU, memory, storage growth, and peak windows.
The recovery or rollback plan if the smaller shape hurts users.
Only the desire to make the bill lower this week.

questions.explanation: Safe tuning combines cost evidence with operating evidence and a way back. A cost target alone is not enough information.
